questions.scenario:
An 8-year-old boy with asthma on inhaled steroid for 2 years. Growth chart as shown(ภาพนี้เลย). His father height 160 cm, mother 150 cm. What is the cause of delayed growth
Chronic asthma
Constitutional growth delay
Familial short stature
Growth hormone deficiency
Chronic use of steroid
A 9-month-old girl คุณแม่มาปรึกษาเรื่องจะกลับไปทำงานแต่ลูกพฤติกรรมเปลี่ยนไป จากเดิมร่าเริงกลายเป็นกลัวคนแปลกหน้า รวมทั้งพี่เลี้ยงด้วย What is the diagnosis
Poor nourishing
Normal development
Anxiety avoidant
Resistant avoidant
A 9-year-old boy has an acute dyspnea. He has underlying disease Tetralogy of Fallot comes with dyspnea and cyanosis  PE: central cyanosis with pan systolic murmur grade 3/6 at apex What is the appropriate management?(ทุกช๊อยเป็นยาหมดเลย)
Morphine
Digitalis
Enalapril
A 3-year-old boy has respiratory distress 2 days. He has history of nonspecific respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago. PE: BT 37C, RR 40/min, PR 130/min, BP 90/50 mmHg, normal S1 S2, gallop, no friction rub, lung crepitation, no splenomegaly, hepatomegaly 4 cm BRCM, span 10 cm. What is the diagnosis
Pericarditis
Viral myocarditis
Infective endocarditis
Acute rheumatic fever
Dilated cardiomyopathy
A 2-year-old girl presented with hoarseness and noisy breathing on inspiring for 2 days which happened after an episode of fever with cough and rhinorrhea. PE: Rhonchi both lung, no crepitation. What is the most appropriate management?
Lateral neck x-ray
Film chest with neck
Inhaled corticosteroid
Salbutamol
Guaifenesin
A 2-month-old infant has loud breathing when sucking. The symptom improved when the infant is in pronation position. PE: BW 5 kg, RR 44, HR 144, inspiratory stridor, harsh breath sound, no wheezing both lungs. What is the most appropriate management?
Reassure
Epinephrine NB
Salbutamol NB
Laryngoscopy
Tracheoscopy
A 6-year-old presents with dyspnea, hoarseness, cough, chest tightness. History of rhinorrhea in the morning. PE: BT 37, RR 30, HR 100, lung-bilateral expiratory wheezing, rhonchi. What is the most likely diagnosis
Asthma
Bacterial tracheitis
Bronchitis acute
Bronchiolitis acute
Pneumonia
An 8-year-old boy, underlying asthma, presents with severe dyspnea. He took salbutamol MDI 2 puff but not improve. PE: suprasternal and subcostal retraction, inspiratory and expiratory wheezing. What is the next management
IV dexamethasone
Albuterol NB
Subcutaneous adrenaline
Subcutaneous terbutaline
IV aminophylline

Laxative
Soap irrigation
Unison enema
Saline irrigate rectum
Rectal stool evacuation

Serum Cl 110
Serum K 4

A 1-year-old boy had a watery diarrhea for 2 days, today he has periodic screaming, crying and also mucous-bloody diarrhea, PE: abdomen- ill defined mass can be palpated at RUQ. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Intussusception
Meckel's diverticulum
Acute gastroenteritis
Midgut volvulus

A 2-year-old presents with hematemesis 4 hours PTA. Physical exams jaundice, hepatomegaly, BP 90/50 mmHg, PR 140/min. What is the next initial management
LPRC 20 cc/kg/hr in 1 hour

9% NSS 20 cc/kg/hr in 30 minutes
Omeprazole
Consult surgery

A 7-year-old patient with hereditary spherocytosis and history of splenectomy comes to the clinic for regular PRBC transfusion. Patient develops severe back pain and severe irritation 2 hours in to the transfusion. What is the best initial management
Give NSAID
Stop transfusion
Start high dose steroid IV
Increase hydration to 250 ml/m2/hr
Rapid infusion to complete transfusion
A 10-year-old girl presents with drowsiness and hyperpnea 2 hours PTA. She has polyuria and polydipsia for 1 month. PE: BT 37 C, PR 70, RR 30, BP 90/70 mmHg, capillary refill 3 sec. Lab: BS 600 mg/dl, Na 125, K 5.5, HCO3 8, Cl 98, urine ketone positive. After fluid resuscite what is the proper management
3%NaCl
Kayexalate

5% NaHCO3
NPH insulin
Regular insulin
A 12-year-old female presents with vagina moniliasis. Physical exams weight 60 kg, height 158 cm, BP 140/90 mmHg, PR 80/min, positive acanthosis nigrican. Lab BS 394 mg/dL, LDL 290, HDL 40, triglyceride 301. Urine glucose 4+, ketone negative. What is the most appropriate management
Biguanide
Insulin
Biguanide and insulin
Life style modification
Fluid replacement dehydration 5%

close school for 7 days
close his classroom for 7 days
oral acyclovir for all student in the classroom
oral acyclovir for student who never receive varicella vaccine

RSV
Streptococcus group A
Coxsackic A
Influenza virus

Wear mask
Wear glove

Immunization

Shigella
Rotavirus
Salmonella spp.
Vibrio cholera

A 5-year-old boy presents with fever for 2 days. Today afebrile with abrupt appear of maculopapular rash at trunk. What is the most likely diagnosis
Measles
Rubella
Roseola infantum
Enterovirus

Tinea faciae
Acne vulgaris

A 7-year-old boy presents with abdominal pain 1 day. He has had rash and arthralgia for 1 week. BT 37, Hct 38%, stool RBC positive. What is the appropriate management
ATB
Steroid

After, she has a red  and tender at her left ankle. PE: BT 38 C, RR 20, normal S1S2, left ankle red and tender with limit of motion. What is the most appropriate investigation for diagnosis
Chest X ray
Echocardiography
Electrocardiography
Antistreptolysin-O
ESR
An 18-year-old woman presents with edema fever 2 days. She has this symptom 2 years ago. PE: pustule at leg. What is the diagnosis
Lupus nephritis
IgA nephropathy
Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis
A 6-year-old girl with rash at inside of her elbow and knee. She was treated with 0.02% TA cream. The rashes subsided, but recur with discontinuation. On physical examination, vesicles on brown hyperpigmented, erythematous skin with excoriation marks at cubital and popliteal fossa. What is the diagnosis
Psoriasis
Atopic dermatitis
Impetigo contagiosum
A child has fever and take medication. After that he develops skin desquamation at face, genitalia. What is the diagnosis
Steven Johnson syndrome
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
A child has history of carbamazepine for 2 weeks. He develops fever. PE: BT 38.5, bilateral conjunctivitis, target lesion at head trunk extremities. Nicolsky sign positive. Skin detachment 35% BSA. What is the most likely diagnosis
Erythema multiforme
Steven Johnson syndrome
Toxic epidermal necrolysis
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
A 2-month-old child with rash both cheeks, scalp 1 month on and off. Mother has allergic rhinitis. PE: dry itchy erythematous plaque with scale and yellow crust. What is the diagnosis
Atopic dermatitis
Seborrheic dermatitis
Tinea faceii
Contact dermatitis
Impetigo contagiosa
A 7-year-old boy came to the ER because Malayan pit viper bite 1 hour PTA. Physical exam fank marks, swelling. He has no dyspnea, no ptosis. Venous blood clotting at 25 min. What is the most appropriate management
Discharge home
Repeat venous clotting time 6 hours
Admit, repeat venous clotting 2 hours
Admit, bed rest, observe neuro signs
Admit, emergency antivenom and repeat venous clotting time
A 10-year-old boy comes with his parents. His teacher told that has multiple staring, not answer the teacher for 10 seconds daily. He has no memory of what happened. PE: no neurological deficit. What is the diagnosis
Absence seizure
Confusional states
Complex partial seizures
A 1-year-old boy on queuing list of TOF operation. He has had fever treat with ATB but not improve 2 weeks. Today he is right hemiplegia. Neck stiffness positive. What is the diagnosis
Meningitis
Encephalitis
Brain abscess
A boy fever 12 days, elbow swelling redness 5 days. Physical exams systolic murmur grade 2/6 at LPSB. Elevated ASO titer. What is the most likely diagnosis
Rheumatic fever
Rheumatoid arthritis
Infective endocarditis
A child with myalgia, headache and high fever. Physical exams reveals mild hepatomegaly. Lab WBC 2,000 (N40%, L30%, M20%, AL 10%), platelet 70,000. What is the next investigation
NS-1 antigen

An infant weight 1,400 g GA 31 weeks presents with dyspnea, cyanosis under FiO2 0.3 Physical exams BT 36.4oC, HR 142/min, RR 68/min, occasional grunting, nasal flaring, coarse crepitation both lung. What is your management
Increase FiO2, ABG
Give surfactant
Radient warmer. Assisted ventilation
Radient warmer, CXR, blood sugar
Septic work-up, ABG
A term male newborn ceasarean section has respiratory distress. BT 37, RR38/min, BP 90/60, PR 140/min, O2 sat 94% room air. PE: no adventitious sound, flare alar nasi, mild supraclavicular retraction. CXR: mild hyperaerate infiltrate bilateral mild effusion both lungs. What is the diagnosis
Acute respiratory distress
Tetralogy of Fallot
Patent ductusarteriosus

An 10-hour newborn, term, has jaundice. BT 37 C, mark jaundice, other normal. Hct 38%,A, Rh positive, microbilirubin 10. Mother: O, Rh positive. Direct coomb test weakly positive. Indirect coomb test negative. What is the diagnosis

total blood transfusion
partial blood transfusion

After prescribing 10%D/W 2 ml/kg push with glucose infusion rate 6 mg/kg/hr and maintenance with rate 65 ml/kg/day. What is the most appropriate next step management

A 3-day-old baby presents with jaundice and frustrated. Physical exams sunken eye balls weight 2800 g, birth weight 3000 g. Mother complains of no breast milk. She has only colostrums. Baby denied feeding. Lab bilirubin 12 mg/dL, others normal. What should you do
Phototherapy
Increase oral fluid
Send her to breast feeding teaching
A 10-day-old neonatal, birth weight 3,200 g, presents with jaundice. She is exclusively breastfed and passes loose yellow stool 6-7 times per day. PE: weight 4,000 g, mild to moderate jaundice, otherwise normal. Lab: Hct 48%, microbillirubin 16 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate management
